column2: Answer
undefined = variable declared but not assigned (or missing param/property). null = explicitly set to "no value." Both are falsy; null == undefined is true; null === undefined is false.
'object' — a historical bug in JS, unfixable for backward compat.

column1: What is NaN and how do you check for it?
column2: Not-a-Number, result of invalid numeric ops. typeof NaN === 'number'. Check with Number.isNaN(x), not isNaN(x) (which coerces first).
No — it means the binding can't be reassigned. The object the const points to is still mutable.
Accessing a let/const/class variable before its declaration line is executed.
Functions, DOM nodes, class methods (prototype chain), Error causes in some runtimes.

column1: Microtask vs macrotask — give one example of each.
column2: Microtask: Promise.then, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver. Macrotask: setTimeout, setInterval, MessageChannel, I/O callbacks.
Yes — await 42 wraps it in Promise.resolve(42). It still yields one microtask tick.
race resolves/rejects with first settled (including rejection). any resolves with first *fulfilled*, only rejects if all reject (AggregateError).
Both coerce to "" (via .toString()), string concatenation → "".

column1: What is a Symbol used for?
column2: Unique, non-enumerable keys; well-known symbols (Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toPrimitive) hook into language protocols.
for...in iterates *enumerable string keys* (including inherited). for...of iterates *values* of any iterable (array, Map, Set, generator). Never use for...in on arrays.
An object with no prototype — no toString, no hasOwnProperty. Used for pure hash maps to avoid prototype pollution attacks.
Evaluates expression, returns undefined. void 0 is a reliable way to get undefined (before ES5, undefined was reassignable).

column1: How does tree-shaking know what to remove?
column2: Static import/export analysis at bundle time. Side-effect-free marking in package.json ("sideEffects": false) tells bundlers they can safely drop unused exports.

column1: What is the difference between currying and partial application?
column2: Currying converts a multi-argument function into a chain of unary functions (f(a)(b)(c)). Partial application fixes one or more arguments up front and returns a function awaiting the rest — it doesn't enforce one-arg-at-a-time.

column1: What breaks the generic curry(fn) implementation based on fn.length?
column2: Rest parameters (...args) and default parameters — both cause fn.length to report 0 or fewer than expected, so the arity check never triggers and the function never executes.

column1: Polyfill vs shim vs transpile — one-line each?
column2: Polyfill = JS code that implements a *missing* runtime API at runtime. Shim = any compatibility layer (may patch broken, not just absent, behavior). Transpile = build-time syntax conversion (arrow → function) — cannot add new APIs.

column1: What is the difference between call and apply?
column2: Both invoke a function with explicit this. call(ctx, arg1, arg2) takes args spread; apply(ctx, [arg1, arg2]) takes args as an array.

column1: What does new do under the hood?
column2: Creates empty object, sets [[Prototype]] to constructor's .prototype, calls constructor with this = new object, returns that object (unless constructor returns a different object).

column1: Structural vs nominal typing in one sentence?
column2: TS checks shape, not name — two different types with the same shape are mutually assignable.

column1: When does never appear in a union?
column2: It disappears — string | never = string.
unknown in strict mode — must narrow before accessing .message.

column1: What does -? do in a mapped type?
column2: Removes the optional modifier, making properties required.
No — unions require type.

column1: What is declaration merging used for?
column2: Module augmentation, extending third-party types (e.g., Express.Request).
Validate an object against a type while retaining narrow literal types.
as const is compile-time only; freeze is runtime. They compose.
Adds implicit children, breaks generic components, returns JSX.Element not ReactNode.
ReturnType<typeof fn> = Promise<T>. Use Awaited<ReturnType<typeof fn>> for the resolved type.
noUncheckedIndexedAccess.
Naked type parameters in union position.
Tuple wrapper prevents distribution.
Only in the extends clause of a conditional type.
{ x?: string } no longer allows x: undefined; only x: string or the property absent.

column1: Method shorthand vs arrow property for variance?
column2: Arrow property is contravariant under strictFunctionTypes; method shorthand is bivariant.
Zero — erased at compile time.

column1: Why prefer z.infer over a separate type definition?
column2: Single source of truth — the schema is the type; no drift.
Infers the narrowest literal type for the argument without requiring as const at call sites.
Ensures each file is independently transpilable — required by esbuild, Babel, SWC; catches const enum and namespace export issues.
A JavaScript representation of the DOM that React diffs between renders to minimize real DOM mutations.
No — state updates are batched and applied asynchronously. React 18 batches all updates (including those in timeouts/promises) by default.
The algorithm React uses to diff the previous and current virtual DOM trees and produce minimal DOM updates. Key prop is the primary identity hint.

column1: Can you use hooks in class components?
column2: No. Hooks are exclusively for function components.

column1: What does key do when it changes?
column2: React unmounts the old component and mounts a new one — all state is destroyed.

column1: What is the difference between defaultProps and default parameter values?
column2: defaultProps is a class/function static property (deprecated for function components in React 19). Default parameters in the function signature are idiomatic modern React.
When its own state changes, when its parent re-renders (unless memoized), or when a consumed context value changes.
A component that returns the same output for the same props and has no side effects during render. React.memo is the function component equivalent of PureComponent.
Wraps a component to skip re-rendering if props are shallowly equal to the previous render. It's an optimization, not a guarantee.

column1: Can event handlers access stale state?
column2: Yes — closures in event handlers capture the state from the render they were created in. Use the functional update form setState(prev => ...) to avoid this.
A form element whose value is entirely driven by React state.
Like useEffect but fires synchronously after DOM mutations, before the browser paints. Use for measuring DOM layout.

column1: What is the exhaustive-deps lint rule checking?
column2: That every variable captured in a useEffect/useMemo/useCallback callback is listed as a dependency.

column1: Can you put an async function directly in useEffect?
column2: No. useEffect must return undefined or a cleanup function. Async functions return Promises. Define async inside the effect and call it.

column1: What happens if you return a non-function from useEffect?
column2: React throws in development. Only undefined or a function is valid.

column1: Why is useLayoutEffect called after mutations but "before paint"?
column2: It runs in the same synchronous flush as the DOM write, before the browser compositor has a chance to render the updated DOM.

column1: What does useId guarantee that Math.random() in render does not?
column2: Hydration consistency — the server and client generate the same ID for the same component instance in the tree.
Yes, guaranteed by React, same as the setState setter.

column1: What is the "tearing" problem in concurrent mode?
column2: Multiple renders reading different snapshots of an external mutable store, resulting in UI inconsistency within a single committed render.

column1: Can useMemo return a different value without dependencies changing?
column2: Yes, React may discard memoized values as an optimization. Never rely on memo for semantic guarantees.

column1: Difference between null and undefined as a dependency?
column2: Object.is(null, undefined) is false — React treats them as different values and will re-run the effect on the transition.
Reverts to the actual state passed to it. The optimistic value is a UI overlay, not committed state.
Labels a custom hook in React DevTools; it is a no-op in production and must only be called inside custom hooks.
Yes. In Concurrent Mode, React may render (call component functions) and then discard the work-in-progress tree if a higher-priority update arrives.

column1: What does Object.is return for two [] literals?
column2: false — they are different object references.
No. React may discard memoized values in low-memory situations (future behavior). It is an optimization hint, not a guarantee.

column1: What triggers a context consumer to re-render?
column2: The context *value* reference changing at the nearest Provider. React.memo does not block context-driven re-renders.
No — hooks must be called at the top level of a component function, not inside callbacks.

column1: What is the difference between bailout and no-op render?
column2: Bailout: React skips calling the component entirely (via memo or Object.is). No-op render: React calls the component, produces identical output, and skips DOM mutations — more expensive than a bailout.

column1: Does changing a key always cause a full remount?
column2: Yes. A key change means a different identity to React — the previous fiber is unmounted (effects cleaned up) and a fresh fiber is mounted.

column1: What fires during the "passive effects" phase?
column2: useEffect callbacks and their cleanup functions from the previous render.

column1: What is the difference between staleTime and gcTime in TanStack Query?
column2: staleTime controls when data is considered outdated and eligible for background refetch. gcTime controls when the inactive cache entry is deleted from memory.

column1: Why is Context not a state manager?
column2: Context is a transport mechanism. It has no built-in subscriptions — consumers re-render on every value change regardless of whether they use the changed slice.

column1: What makes a Zustand selector cause unnecessary re-renders?
column2: Returning a new object reference from the selector on every call. Use useShallow or select primitive values.

column1: When would you pick Jotai over Zustand?
column2: When state is naturally per-entity and independent (e.g., per-row in a table), or when you want first-class Suspense integration at the atom level.

column1: Can you use TanStack Query and Zustand together?
column2: Yes. TanStack Query handles server state; Zustand handles client UI state that is not tied to a server resource. They complement each other cleanly.
A utility that generates a normalized { ids: [], entities: {} } structure and pre-built CRUD reducers (add, update, remove, upsert) plus selectors, eliminating O(n) array scans.

column1: How do you persist Zustand state across page reloads?
column2: The persist middleware wraps the store and syncs it to localStorage (or any custom storage adapter) automatically.

column1: What is the enabled option in useQuery for?
column2: To conditionally skip a query — for example, waiting for a dependent value like a user ID before fetching user-specific data.

column1: How does RTK's Immer integration work?
column2: RTK wraps reducers in Immer's produce. You can write "mutating" code against the state draft; Immer produces a new immutable state object using structural sharing.
A library that provides a useState-compatible API over URL search params, with type-safe serialization and batched updates, for Next.js and other frameworks.

column1: What's the first thing you check when a React app is slow?
column2: React DevTools Profiler — how many components are re-rendering per interaction and what's triggering them.

column1: Does React.memo prevent re-renders if a prop is a function?
column2: No. Functions are recreated each render, producing new references. Pair memo with useCallback (or React Compiler).

column1: What replaced FID in Core Web Vitals?
column2: INP (Interaction to Next Paint), in March 2024. INP measures full interaction latency, not just the delay before the first handler.
Re-exporting everything through an index.ts can prevent tree-shaking, causing unused code to appear in the bundle.
When the computation is cheaper than the comparison React does to decide whether to recompute. Anything under ~1ms on fast hardware is likely not worth memoizing manually.

column1: How does Streaming SSR improve performance?
column2: It flushes an HTML shell immediately (good TTFB), then streams Suspense boundaries as data resolves (good LCP), without waiting for all async data before sending any bytes.

column1: What does the React Compiler change about memo?
column2: It auto-applies memoization equivalent to memo/useMemo/useCallback for components that follow the Rules of React — removing the need for most manual memoization.

column1: How do you detect a memory leak in a React app?
column2: Chrome DevTools Memory tab → Heap Snapshot before/after repeated interaction → look for growing retained size and detached DOM nodes.

column1: What's the difference between staleTime and gcTime in TanStack Query?
column2: staleTime: how long data is considered fresh (no refetch). gcTime: how long unused cache entries stay in memory before garbage collection.

column1: When would you use a Web Worker in a React app?
column2: CPU-intensive work that would block the main thread for >50ms — e.g., parsing large CSV, running ML inference, complex data transformation.
Marks a module boundary. All code in the file and its imports run on the client. Server Components can still render as children via composition.
Yes — layout.tsx can be an async Server Component.
307 vs 308 HTTP status. Use permanent for SEO-friendly moves.
A way to cache arbitrary async functions (not just fetch) in the Data Cache.

column1: How do you access search params in a Server Component?
column2: Via the searchParams prop on page.tsx — it's a plain object, not a URL instance.

column1: What breaks if you use Math.random() in a Server Component?
column2: Static rendering will fail — it's non-deterministic. Use export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'.
Remounting on navigation — entry animations, per-page analytics events, form state reset.

column1: How do you share state between parallel routes?
column2: URL state (search params) or a shared parent context via a Client Component layout.
Yes — remote images must be configured in next.config.ts under images.remotePatterns.
cookies(), headers(), searchParams, fetch with no-store, Date.now(), Math.random().
getBy throws on missing; queryBy returns null — use it for asserting absence.
It adds no a11y signal and creates test coupling to markup details. Use role + name.
RTL's bound query object that searches the full rendered document — preferred over destructuring from render().
Use afterEach(() => server.resetHandlers()) to avoid handler state leaking between tests.

column1: How do you test a useEffect that runs on mount?
column2: Render the component, assert on the side effect's output (DOM change, mock call) — do not test the effect directly.
Jest requires transform config for ESM modules; Vitest handles them natively via Vite.
Use findBy when waiting for an element to appear; use waitFor when waiting for an assertion on an already-present element to become true.

column1: What does retry: false in QueryClient do in tests?
column2: Prevents React Query from retrying failed requests, avoiding timeout-induced act() warnings.
Coverage = lines executed; mutation score = percentage of deliberate code mutations your tests catch. Mutation score is the stronger signal.
A ZIP file of screenshots, network logs, and DOM snapshots for a test run — invaluable for debugging CI flakiness locally.
noopener prevents the new tab from accessing window.opener (phishing via tab hijack). noreferrer suppresses the Referrer header and implies noopener.

column1: Is CORS a security mechanism on the server?
column2: No. CORS is browser-enforced. Curl ignores it. It restricts cross-origin JS from reading responses, not from making requests.

column1: Can an httpOnly cookie be stolen via XSS?
column2: Not directly read. But XSS can trigger authenticated requests using the cookie (session riding), exfiltrate CSRF tokens, or modify the page to harvest credentials.

column1: What is SameSite=Lax vs Strict for OAuth?
column2: Strict breaks OAuth redirect flows because the redirect from the IdP is cross-site. Lax allows top-level navigational GETs, making it compatible with OAuth redirects while still blocking CSRF on state-changing requests.

column1: What is a postinstall script risk?
column2: npm runs lifecycle scripts at install time. A malicious package can exfiltrate env vars, keys in the environment, or modify local files. Use npm ci --ignore-scripts in CI.

column1: How do Trusted Types prevent XSS?
column2: They make DOM XSS sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval) require a special TrustedHTML/TrustedScript object that can only be produced by explicitly declared policies, enforced by the browser.
A integrity attribute on <script> or <link> tags with a cryptographic hash. The browser blocks execution if the fetched file does not match the hash, defending against CDN compromise.

column1: What is prototype pollution and which JS operation commonly triggers it?
column2: Deeply merging { "__proto__": { "isAdmin": true } } into a plain object mutates Object.prototype, affecting every object. Lodash merge historically was vulnerable; use Object.create(null) for data maps.
They are statically inlined into the client bundle at build time. Anything you put in NEXT_PUBLIC_ is visible to anyone who loads your page and inspects the JS. Never use it for secrets.

column1: How do you handle CORS for a public API vs a private API?
column2: Public API: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * without credentials is fine. Private API: whitelist specific origins, require credentials, validate every request with authentication.

Tells AT that content outside the dialog should be treated as inert. Without it, VoiceOver users can navigate outside a modal via the virtual cursor. Note: browser support for actually inert-ing the background is inconsistent — pair with inert attribute on background content.
An HTML attribute that makes an element and all its descendants non-interactive and hidden from AT. Use on background content when a modal is open.

column1: What is the accessible name for <img src="logo.png" />?
column2: The file name ("logo.png") — always provide alt.
role="presentation" removes element semantics but keeps descendants visible to AT. aria-hidden="true" removes the element and all descendants from the tree entirely.
Yes, with tabIndex={0}. But you also need role, onKeyDown for Enter/Space, and all state management — always prefer a native element.
A new property (ARIA 1.2) equivalent to inline aria-describedby without requiring a separate element with an ID. AT support is still maturing.

column1: What is the difference between role="button" and type="submit"?
column2: None from a role perspective — both expose role="button". The difference is behavior: type="submit" triggers form submission; type="button" does not.

column1: What CSS property is used to visually hide content while keeping it in the accessibility tree?
column2: The "visually-hidden" utility: position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0,0,0,0); white-space: nowrap;. Do NOT use display: none or visibility: hidden.
The default; region changes are not announced. Useful to explicitly disable live behavior on a container that contains a live region descendant.

column1: Lighthouse a11y score of 100 means WCAG AA compliant?
column2: No. Automated tools cover ~30-40% of criteria. A score of 100 means axe found no automated violations; it says nothing about color meaning, logical heading structure quality, or meaningful alt text.

column1: When would you choose SSE over WebSocket?
column2: When communication is server → client only. SSE is simpler, uses HTTP/2 multiplexing, and auto-reconnects. WebSocket only needed for bidirectional.

column1: What is staleTime vs gcTime in React Query?
column2: staleTime: how long data is considered fresh (no background refetch). gcTime: how long unused cache entries are kept before garbage collection.

column1: How do you prevent waterfall data fetching in React?
column2: Lift fetches to route-level loaders (React Router v6, Next.js), use parallel queries, or use RSC which can fetch in parallel on the server.

column1: What is CLS and how do you prevent it?
column2: Cumulative Layout Shift — elements moving after initial render. Prevent by reserving space for images (aspect-ratio), skeleton screens with fixed dimensions, avoiding DOM insertions above the fold.
Offset is simple but unstable (inserts shift pages). Cursor is stable and performant. Always use cursor for infinite scroll.

column1: How do you handle a design token change that breaks 30 components?
column2: Update the token, run visual regression in Chromatic (Storybook), review diffs, then release as a minor version if additive or major version if breaking. Consumers pin and upgrade on their schedule.

column1: What is Module Federation's biggest operational risk?
column2: Version mismatch of shared singletons (React). If host and remote ship different React versions and singleton: true is not set, you get two React instances and hook failures.
Situation, Task, Action, Result, Lesson.

column1: What are the three pillars of observability?
column2: Logs, metrics, traces.

column1: What does a blameless postmortem produce?
column2: Timeline, contributing factors (no blame), action items, runbook updates.
The acceptable amount of unreliability given an SLO target.
Would this person make those around them better?

column1: Name the four layers of a …
column2: Name the four layers of a good code review. Correctness, design, readability, security/performance.

column1: How do you say "it depends" at the senior level?
column2: State the axes: "It depends on X, Y, and Z — given [assumption], I would choose..."

column1: What do take-home reviewers look for beyond correctness?
column2: Accessibility, error handling, testing strategy, README reasoning, what was skipped and why.

column1: What makes a question to the interviewer signal-rich?
column2: It surfaces something you cannot Google and reveals you know what senior work actually looks like.

column1: What is the first thing you do in a live coding session?
column2: Clarify the problem and state your constraints.
Know your target range before the call, give a range not a point, negotiate total comp not just base.
Name it, make it visible, present the trade-off explicitly.
Replacing a system incrementally — new functionality goes into the new system while the old system handles existing paths.

column1: What is the cost-of-inaction argument for tech debt?
column2: Quantify bug-fix hours attributable to the debt vs. the cost of the refactor.
Optimistic / most likely / pessimistic — commit to most likely, communicate the range.
An HOC wraps a component; a hook wraps logic — one adds a component layer, one does not.
displayName on HOCs is mandatory; without it DevTools shows stacked anonymous wrappers.
React.Children.map breaks on fragments — Context is the correct compound-component approach today.

column1: Render props are still valid when …
column2: Render props are still valid when the consumer needs to own the markup of a specific slot.

column1: The state reducer pattern inverts control …
column2: The state reducer pattern inverts control over *how* state transitions happen, not just *when*.

column1: Control props require you to detect controlled vs uncontrolled mode …
column2: Control props require you to detect controlled vs uncontrolled mode — never toggle between them.

column1: Provider context values should be memoized …
column2: Provider context values should be memoized or split by update frequency to prevent cascade re-renders.

column1: Headless UI separates behavior/a11y from styling …
column2: Headless UI separates behavior/a11y from styling — the right trade for design-system longevity.

column1: children as a prop is stable across parent renders if the parent doesn't re-render …
column2: children as a prop is stable across parent renders if the parent doesn't re-render — use it for perf.

column1: The container/presentational split is organizational choice, …
column2: The container/presentational split is organizational choice, not architectural requirement, post-hooks.
Custom hooks should follow single-responsibility — one concern per hook, compose upward.
Tuple returns favor rename-ability; object returns favor selective destructuring.

column1: as / polymorphic props are a consumer convenience, not a styling system …
column2: as / polymorphic props are a consumer convenience, not a styling system — don't overload them.

column1: Provider hell is solved by co-locating …
column2: Provider hell is solved by co-locating providers with their feature, not stacking all at root.

column1: Compound components with Context support arbitrary nesting depth …
column2: Compound components with Context support arbitrary nesting depth; cloneElement does not.

column1: A discriminated union for state (`{ status …
column2: A discriminated union for state ({ status: "loading" | "error" | "success" }) prevents impossible states better than multiple booleans.

column1: Prop getters (e.g., getToggleProps) merge consumer event handlers with internal ones …
column2: Prop getters (e.g., getToggleProps) merge consumer event handlers with internal ones — always call-through, never clobber.
React.memo is itself an HOC; wrapping it with another HOC requires careful ref and displayName handling.

column1: Headless libraries own ARIA and keyboard behavior …
column2: Headless libraries own ARIA and keyboard behavior — prefer them over hand-rolling for accessibility compliance.

column1: The best pattern is the one …
column2: The best pattern is the one your team can read, extend, and delete without a comment explaining why it exists.

column1: Fetch rejects on network error or HTTP error?
column2: Network error only. Check res.ok for HTTP errors.
Data is immediately stale — TanStack Query will background-refetch on every mount and focus. Default behavior.
A let cancelled = false variable in useEffect that prevents state updates from stale async callbacks after cleanup runs.

column1: SSE vs WebSocket for a notification feed?
column2: SSE — unidirectional, simpler, automatic reconnect, HTTP compatible.

column1: How do you prevent a mutation from running twice on double-click?
column2: mutation.isPending — disable the button while a mutation is in flight.
Cache entries are removed immediately when all subscribers unmount — effectively disables caching.
After a successful mutation when you already have the updated server response — avoids a redundant refetch.

column1: What HTTP method should retries be careful with?
column2: POST — not idempotent. Use idempotency keys or only retry on network errors, not on response errors.
A Suspense boundary and either use() (React 19), useSuspenseQuery, or a Suspense-compatible library.

column1: How do you share a single WebSocket across components?
column2: Via a singleton (module-level or context), not per-component useEffect. Feed updates through useSyncExternalStore.

column1: What is the difference between <Link> and <a>?
column2: <Link> intercepts clicks and uses pushState; <a> triggers a full page reload.

column1: What does replace do in navigate('/path', { replace: true })?
column2: Replaces the current history entry instead of pushing a new one — the back button skips over it.
A child route with no path that renders at the parent's exact URL — the default child.

column1: Why must you wrap <Suspense> around lazy routes?
column2: React throws a Promise on first render of a lazy component; Suspense catches it and shows the fallback until the chunk resolves.

column1: What is the difference between errorElement and a try/catch in a loader?
column2: errorElement renders when the loader throws or rejects; try/catch lets the loader return a partial result instead of blowing up the route.
Yes — sibling route loaders run concurrently; child loaders start only after their parent loader resolves (unless you defer).
NavLink receives an isActive / isPending callback so you can apply active styles without manual location comparison.
Matches any path not caught by earlier routes — use it for 404 pages.

column1: Can you use React Router with React Native?
column2: Not directly — use react-router-native or React Navigation for mobile.
Accesses the error thrown in a loader, action, or child component inside an errorElement.
Good for enforcing public API boundaries. Bad when they include large unused exports that prevent tree-shaking. Use with bundler analysis.

column1: When does a component belong in shared vs a feature?
column2: Shared when 3+ features need it and it has no domain knowledge. Otherwise, start in the feature.

column1: How do you prevent shared from becoming a dumping ground?
column2: Contribution gate: PR requires 2 reviewers from different teams, no domain types allowed.

column1: Monorepo vs polyrepo for a team of 8?
column2: Monorepo with Turborepo if you have more than one app sharing code. Polyrepo if apps are truly independent.
Worth it for teams 15+ engineers who will invest in tooling and education. For smaller teams, by-feature with a shared layer delivers the same result with less ceremony.

column1: Micro frontends for a team of 20?
column2: Almost certainly no. Build a well-structured monorepo first.
In shared/flags/ behind a typed hook. Features never import the flag SDK directly.

column1: How do you enforce module boundaries in CI?
column2: eslint-plugin-boundaries fails the lint step on invalid imports. dependency-cruiser generates a visual graph and can be configured to fail on rule violations.

column1: Storybook vs unit tests — which do you choose?
column2: Both serve different purposes. Storybook catches visual and a11y regressions; unit tests catch logic errors. Interaction tests in Storybook can replace some integration tests for UI flows.

column1: How do you handle breaking changes to a shared component?
column2: Semantic versioning + Changesets for changelog. Codemods for automated migration when possible. Deprecation period with console warnings before removal.
DOMContentLoaded fires when HTML is parsed and deferred scripts run. load fires after all resources (images, fonts, iframes) are fetched.

column1: Does async or defer help the preload scanner?
column2: Both — the preload scanner discovers <script src> regardless, but async/defer prevents execution from blocking the parser.

column1: What's the cheapest CSS animation property?
column2: opacity and transform — both are compositor-only and skip layout and paint.

column1: Can you read localStorage from a service worker?
column2: No — service workers have no access to localStorage or sessionStorage. Use IndexedDB or Cache API.
Non-urgent work after paint: analytics, pre-fetching, non-critical hydration. Not appropriate for time-sensitive or visual work.
Each will-change can create a compositor layer consuming GPU memory. Promoting thousands of elements exhausts VRAM on low-end devices.
The UDP-based transport layer under HTTP/3. Eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking and enables 0-RTT reconnection, improving performance on mobile/lossy networks.

column1: What triggers a reflow but NOT a repaint?
column2: Nothing — reflow always causes at least a repaint of affected areas. The ordering is unidirectional: layout → paint → composite.

column1: SameSite=Lax vs Strict for auth cookies?
column2: Lax allows cookies on top-level GET navigations (e.g., clicking a link) — good UX. Strict blocks all cross-site sending — maximum CSRF protection but users lose session when arriving from external links.
preload: current page, high priority, mandatory fetch. prefetch: future navigation, idle priority, speculative.

column1: What's the difference between a loader and a plugin in Webpack?
column2: Loaders transform individual file types; plugins operate on the full compilation via Webpack's tapable hooks.
It makes all .js files in that package ESM. CJS files must then be named .cjs.
Prefetch fetches the chunk during browser idle for *future* navigation; preload fetches it in parallel with the current chunk (use for things needed on current page).

column1: esbuild is faster than Babel — why don't we use it everywhere?
column2: esbuild has no plugin API equivalent to Babel's, no type checking, and less mature code splitting. It's used where speed matters most (transforms, pre-bundling).
Rollup/Webpack merge small module bodies into a single scope instead of wrapping each in a function, reducing closure overhead and enabling better minification.

column1: What does sideEffects: false in package.json mean?
column2: Tells bundlers that importing unused exports from this package has no global side effects, enabling full tree shaking of unused modules.

column1: How does Vite differ in dev vs prod?
column2: Dev: no bundling, native ESM served on demand, esbuild transforms. Prod: Rollup bundle with full optimization.
A Rust-based Webpack replacement with API compatibility — same config, dramatically faster builds.

column1: How do you prevent source maps from leaking proprietary code?
column2: Use hidden-source-map, upload to error monitoring service via CI, exclude .map files from CDN deployment.

column1: What breaks tree shaking most often in a real codebase?
column2: Barrel files doing export * from across large component libraries, combined with packages missing "sideEffects": false.

column1: What is a lockfile and why must CI use it?
column2: A lockfile pins exact dependency versions. --frozen-lockfile / --ci fails the build if the lockfile is out of sync with package.json, ensuring reproducible installs.

column1: What is OIDC in GitHub Actions?
column2: OpenID Connect lets runners assume cloud IAM roles using a short-lived JWT instead of storing long-lived secret keys.
Cancels the running workflow for the same branch when a new commit is pushed. Saves runner minutes on rapid-fire commits.
A reusable GitHub Actions unit defined in a repo (action.yml) that bundles multiple steps. Lighter weight than a reusable workflow, no separate runner.

column1: What is a required status check?
column2: A CI job that must pass before a PR can be merged, enforced by branch protection rules in GitHub repository settings.

column1: Difference between vars and secrets in GitHub Actions?
column2: vars are plaintext config (API URLs, feature flag keys). secrets are encrypted and masked in logs (tokens, passwords).
The transition from old to new version happens as a single swap, never leaving the system in a partial state where some files are old and some are new.
Immutable cache forever. Changing content generates a new hash, so the CDN serves the new file automatically without cache invalidation.
Routing a small percentage of traffic to the new version to validate before full rollout.
Accumulation of stale feature flags that are permanently enabled or disabled but never removed from the codebase.
A build system for JavaScript/TypeScript monorepos that caches task outputs by content hash and runs tasks in dependency order, skipping unchanged work.
Analyzes the build output and fails CI if the JavaScript bundle exceeds a defined budget in bytes.
A markdown file in a PR that describes the changes and their semver impact (patch/minor/major), consumed by the Changesets tool to automate versioning and changelogs.
A named version in Sentry associated with a source map upload and deploy event, used to scope errors to specific deploys and compare error rates across releases.
State change, parent re-render, or context value change.
One memoizes a value, the other a function reference — same mechanism.
Microtask queue drains fully before the next macrotask runs.
Use functional updater setState(prev => ...) or a ref for the latest value.
any disables type checking; unknown requires a narrowing check before use.
RSC: zero client JS, async data, no hooks. Client: interactive, hooks, browser APIs.
Causes React to destroy and recreate fibers on list reorder, losing local state.
Every consumer re-renders on any value change; use external store for high-frequency state.
Return a function to abort fetches or unsubscribe — prevents memory leaks and race conditions.

column1: TanStack Query vs Redux for server data
column2: Server state is a caching problem, not a state problem — Query handles staleness, refetch, dedup.
The legal-compliance target; A is minimum, AAA is aspirational.
Auto-escaped in JSX; dangerouslySetInnerHTML and href with javascript: are the gaps.
Cookie with HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict is invisible to JS; localStorage is XSS-vulnerable.
SSR renders per request (fresh, slower); SSG renders at build time (fast, can be stale).
Reading DOM layout before paint to prevent visual flicker — otherwise use useEffect.
Debounce fires after silence; throttle fires at a steady rate.
React.lazy + Suspense at route boundaries — free and highest impact.
More integration tests than unit tests for frontend; e2e for critical paths only.
They solve org/team autonomy problems, not engineering problems; default to monolith.
Decouples deployment from release, enabling gradual rollout and instant rollback.
